food_name: Taiwanese Beef Noodle Soup (Hong Shao Niu Rou Mian)
food_subgroup: Wheat noodle soup with red-braised beef tendon and brisket in soy-doubanjiang broth

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for Taiwanese red-braised beef noodle soup. Closest parent is 'beef noodle soup'.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Taiwanese red-braised beef noodle soup. 'Composite dishes' is the closest parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this dish. SNOMED CT food hierarchy does not include Taiwanese regional noodle dishes at this specificity.

main_ingredients: aromatic spices (star anise, cinnamon, bay leaf)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant bowl of Taiwanese beef noodle soup weighing approximately 600g, comprising 120g thick wheat noodles, 180g braised beef tendon and brisket, and 280g rich soy-spice-tomato broth, garnished with bok choy, spring onion, and pickled mustard greens.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beef noodle soup, beef brisket, cooked wheat noodles, and broth components; cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Taiwan composite dish estimation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or FAO/INFOODS entry exists for the complete Taiwanese red-braised beef noodle soup dish. Nutrient values are derived from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked wheat noodles (FDC ID 168936), braised beef brisket (FDC ID 174032), beef tendon (collagen-rich connective tissue, FDC ID 169453), and soy-based braising broth components, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA regional data for Taiwanese beef noodle soup. The per-100g values reflect the blended 600g bowl composition (120g noodles + 180g beef + 280g broth). Sodium value of 620mg/100g (3,720mg/bowl) is consistent with published analyses of Taiwanese restaurant beef noodle soup broth sodium content.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of wheat noodles and mixed beef noodle soup — analogous estimate based on cooked wheat noodles GI data; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Taiwanese red-braised beef noodle soup as a composite dish. GI of ~52 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked wheat noodles (GI ~47–55, mean ~52) as the primary carbohydrate source. The high protein content (~9.5g/100g, ~57g/bowl) and fat content (~4.2g/100g, ~25g/bowl) from braised beef and collagen-rich tendon are well-established moderators of postprandial glucose response, supporting the lower end of the wheat noodle GI range. GL per 600g bowl = GI(52) × available carbohydrate(60g) / 100 = 31.2, rounded to 31.0. This is classified HIGH GL (>20). Reference: Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 620mg/100g; ~3,720mg per 600g bowl — exceeds the 1,500mg daily target for hypertensive patients and the 2,400mg general adult upper limit in a single meal
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should avoid or strictly limit this dish in standard restaurant portions; ordering the clear-broth version (清燉 qīng dùn, without doubanjiang) reduces sodium to approximately 1,800mg per bowl, and requesting reduced broth volume further lowers sodium proportionally — even so, sodium remains very high and the dish should be an occasional choice only.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glycemic load 31 per bowl (HIGH) from 60g available carbohydrate in thick wheat noodles; GI ~52 (low-medium) partially mitigated by high protein (57g/bowl) and fat (25g/bowl)
type2_diabetes.guidance: T2DM patients should request a half-portion of noodles (60g instead of 120g), which reduces carbohydrate to ~30g and GL to approximately 16 (medium); eating the beef and tendon first before the noodles activates protein-first postprandial glucose modulation and further attenuates the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high sodium (~3,720mg/bowl) is the primary CVD risk driver; saturated fat is moderate at 1.5g/100g (~9g/bowl); cholesterol 38mg/100g (~228mg/bowl)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: CVD patients should treat this dish as a high-sodium, occasional food; the clear-broth (qīng dùn) variant with reduced noodle portion and minimal broth consumption is the least harmful option, and the high collagen and protein content from beef tendon and brisket do not offset the cardiovascular risk posed by the sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef tendon collagen (~35g/100g tendon) has joint-health relevance; however, very high sodium activates Th17-mediated inflammation in autoimmune disease
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients may benefit from the collagen and zinc content of beef tendon, but the critically high sodium content is a meaningful pro-inflammatory concern and portion reduction or clear-broth substitution is strongly advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Taiwanese Beef Noodle Soup (紅燒牛肉麵, hong shao niu rou mian) is Taiwan's most internationally recognised dish and is widely regarded as the island's national dish. A standard restaurant bowl (~600g) contains approximately 690 kcal, 57g protein (very high), 25g fat, and 60g carbohydrate, with critically high sodium of approximately 3,720mg per bowl — exceeding the recommended daily sodium limit for hypertensive patients in a single serving. The dish consists of thick, chewy wheat noodles served in a deeply flavoured red-braised broth made from soy sauce, doubanjiang (Sichuan broad bean chili paste), tomatoes, and aromatic spices including star anise and cinnamon. The protein component is braised beef brisket and tendon; beef tendon is one of the richest dietary sources of collagen (~35g collagen per 100g tendon), giving the broth a rich, gelatinous body and the meat a soft, yielding texture. The dish is garnished with bok choy, spring onion, and pickled mustard greens. The GI is estimated at ~52 (low-medium) based on wheat noodle GI data, but the glycemic load per bowl is 31 (HIGH) due to the large noodle portion. The dish is dairy-free but contains gluten and soy. It is not Halal. A clear-broth variant (清燉 qīng dùn) omits doubanjiang and reduces sodium to approximately 1,800mg per bowl. The dish originated with Shandong Chinese military veteran immigrants who settled in Taiwan after 1949 and has been celebrated at the annual Taipei Beef Noodle Festival since 2005. Nutritional data derived from USDA FoodData Central composite estimation cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA regional data.
common_user_queries: hong shao niu rou mian nutrition
is Taiwanese beef noodle soup healthy
how much protein in beef noodle soup Taiwan

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for the component foods (cooked wheat noodles, braised beef brisket, beef tendon, soy-based broth) cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA regional data. The per-100g macros are internally consistent with the stated bowl composition (600g = 120g noodles + 180g beef + 280g broth). Sodium value is consistent with published analyses of Taiwanese restaurant beef noodle soup. GI is estimated from an approved University of Sydney GI Database analogous food (cooked wheat noodles) with documented rationale. Confidence is high for macros and sodium; micronutrient values carry slightly higher uncertainty due to composite estimation.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite Taiwanese braised beef noodle soup dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA at the composite dish level as of 2026; individual component data exist but reliable weighted blending for MUFA/PUFA is not defensible without laboratory analysis. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish (including broth) is not reported as a single figure in available approved sources. trans_fat_g is 0.1g/100g, reflecting trace ruminant trans-fat (vaccenic acid) from beef, consistent with USDA data for braised beef. All core macros (protein_g 9.5, fat_g 4.2, carbohydrate_g 10.0) are non-zero and well-supported; no zero-macro justification required. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database wheat noodle data; no direct GI measurement for the composite dish exists in approved sources. Sodium of 620mg/100g (3,720mg/bowl) is critically high and represents the primary clinical concern for this dish across hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and autoimmune conditions. The dish is NOT Halal (beef may not be Halal-slaughtered in standard Taiwanese restaurants; doubanjiang may contain non-Halal additives). Clear-broth variant (清燉 qīng dùn) is a meaningful sodium-reduction option.
